2026-05-20.notes: Genset supplied 42% of system energy. Afternoon solar curtailed once battery hit 99% at 11:55.
2026-05-20.commentary:
Production on May 20 needs reading carefully. DeyeCloud's headline daily production of 764 kWh blends two very different sources: 446 kWh of actual solar and 318 kWh from the diesel generator. The generator supplied 42% of everything the system delivered that day - a high share for a 96 kWp array, and the single most important number on this page.

The battery itself had a healthy day. SOC bottomed at 38% around 04:45, comfortably above the 20% floor and even above the 28-35% manual-start band, and reached 99% by late morning. Depth of discharge was 61 points and the bank never entered the sub-30% warning zone.

The generator ran 4.92 hours across three windows, costing roughly PHP 13,800 in diesel and delivering energy at about PHP 43 per kWh - nine times the cost of solar. The 04:50 and 20:30 runs caught genuine low points; the 10:20 to 11:05 run did not - it charged the bank from 80% to 95% mid-morning when solar was already climbing. Because the morning runs pushed the battery to 99% by 11:55, the array sat curtailed all afternoon, spilling an estimated 80-90 kWh of free solar. Tomorrow: hold the morning genset to just clearing the pre-dawn low and let solar do the daytime charging.

events.reason: Mid-morning top-up - questionable, solar was already charging

2026-05-21.notes: Auto-captured from DeyeCloud station 61801841. Solar 514 kWh, genset 231 kWh (31% of energy). Genset ran 2 windows, 3.83 hr total.
2026-05-21.commentary:
May 21 was a strong solar day undercut by the same mistake as the day before. The array produced 514 kWh of actual solar - a healthy figure for the season - but the diesel generator still added 231 kWh on top, 31% of the day's energy. On a clear day at roughly 10 rooms occupied, that genset share should be close to zero.

The battery cycled cleanly. It bottomed at 42% just before the evening genset run and never came near the 20% floor; depth of discharge was a comfortable 58 points. No concern on how the bank was treated.

The problem is the 10:00 to 11:20 genset run. It started with the battery already at 66% and climbing on solar, then pushed it to a full 100% by 11:20 - about 1.3 hours and roughly 43 litres of diesel for energy the sun was delivering for free. Filling the bank that early also left no headroom for midday solar, so the array spent the afternoon curtailed. The evening run from 20:30 to 23:00, lifting SOC from 42% to 90%, was the genuinely necessary one. The fix for tomorrow: skip the mid-morning genset entirely and let solar carry the daytime charge.

events.reason: Mid-morning run - charged bank to 100% during solar hours, avoidable

2026-05-22.notes: Auto-captured from DeyeCloud station 61801841. Weak solar day (272 kWh); genset supplied 55% of energy.
2026-05-22.commentary:
May 22 was a weak solar day. The array produced just 272 kWh, little more than half of May 21, so this was almost certainly an overcast or monsoon day. With solar short, the diesel generator carried 333 kWh - 55% of the day's energy. On a genuinely cloudy day heavy genset use is expected, so this is less alarming than it looks, but it is still the most expensive way to power the hotel.

The battery was cycled hard but safely. SOC bottomed at 32%, inside the 28-35% manual-start band and well clear of the 20% floor, with depth of discharge of 67 points. No battery-health concern, though a day this deep is not one you want repeated back to back.

The generator ran 5.75 hours across three windows. The evening run from 18:45 to 22:15, lifting SOC from 33% to 93%, was clearly necessary. The 10:00 to 12:05 run is more defensible than on a sunny day given the weak solar, though it still charged to 95%; stopping nearer 80% leaves room for whatever afternoon sun appears. With a poor solar forecast, plan the evening genset window early and keep the daytime run as short as the low points allow.

2026-05-23.commentary:
May 23 brought reasonable solar production at 410 kWh - about 12% above the Siargao annual-average benchmark of 366 kWh and consistent with recent history. Even so, the diesel generator supplied 281 kWh, a 41% share of total energy. On a day with adequate solar that share should be under 15%, and the culprit is visible in the morning cluster of genset runs.

The battery cycled safely. SOC bottomed at 39%, above the 20% floor and clear of the 28-35% manual-start band, with depth of discharge of 60 points. The bank reached 99.7% float, and the 75% sunset SOC left a healthy buffer heading into the night. No battery health concerns.

The genset ran 4.67 hours, burning roughly 152 litres at an estimated PHP 13,130 - about PHP 47 per kWh delivered. The evening run at 20:00 (44% SOC start) was justified. The morning cluster is the avoidable cost: the first run started at 45% SOC during rising solar and pushed the bank to 95%, followed by two brief top-up stutters at 97% and 99%. Those three runs together spent about 2 hours doing work the sun would have done for free. The rule for tomorrow: hold the morning genset until SOC enters the 28-35% band, and stop it at 75-80% so solar can finish the charge.

2026-05-24.commentary:
May 24 was the strongest production day in the current dataset at 527 kWh, approximately 44% above the annual-average baseline. The genset contributed 222 kWh (30% of total energy), but with only a single run all day, the operational pattern was much cleaner than previous days. There is no evidence of avoidable mid-morning diesel use, which is a meaningful improvement.

Battery cycling was conservative and healthy. SOC reached only 93%, which is not a concern - the array charged the bank to near-full without genset support pushing for float. Min SOC was 34.2%, sitting just inside the 28-35% manual-start band, confirming the evening run was correctly triggered. Depth of discharge was 59 points. The 39% sunset SOC signalled the need for the evening run, which delivered it cleanly.

The single genset run at 18:00 (35% start, 93% stop) ran 3.5 hours at an estimated PHP 9,839 - the lowest daily diesel cost in the log. This is what clean operation looks like: one evening window triggered at a genuine low point, solar doing all the daytime work. The remaining refinement is small: stopping the run at 85% instead of 93% saves roughly 30 minutes of diesel, as morning solar easily restores the difference. If tomorrow has similar solar, this pattern should repeat with no modifications needed.

2026-05-25.commentary:
May 25 showed weaker solar at 378 kWh - about 3% above the annual-average estimate but well below the recent 7-day average, pointing to partial cloud cover. The genset carried 296 kWh, a 44% energy share. While the overcast conditions justify higher genset use than May 24, the morning run pattern pushed fuel costs back up toward the weekly average.

The battery cycled more deeply than recent days. SOC bottomed at 31.5%, inside the 28-35% manual-start band and above the 20% floor, so the 07:40 morning start was correctly triggered by a genuine overnight draw. Depth of discharge was 68 points. Max SOC reached 99.7%, and the 78% sunset SOC was healthy. The 10:25 five-minute micro-run at 99% SOC is a minor anomaly - likely an automatic restart on a transient load; worth noting in the site log.

The morning run from 07:40 to 09:55 charged from 33% to 94%. Stopping at 94% when solar was already climbing cost an extra 30-40 minutes versus a target of 75-80%, adding roughly 35 litres (PHP 3,000) in avoidable diesel. The evening run at 20:00 (48% start, 85% stop) was well-executed. Total estimated cost for the day: PHP 12,170. The one concrete change: on cloudy mornings, still stop the dawn run at 75-80% and let any available afternoon sun push it higher before nightfall.

2026-05-26.commentary:
May 26 delivered 485.2 kWh of solar, about 32% above the annual-average baseline for Siargao, with a respectable 78.92 kW peak. Despite this reasonable harvest, the diesel genset contributed 312.3 kWh out of 797.5 kWh total - a 39% share - indicating that overnight and evening demand significantly eroded stored solar energy before the following morning could replenish it.

Battery cycling covered 55.5 percentage points (91.8% max down to 36.3% min), a moderately deep cycle. The 36.3% minimum SOC landed just above the 28-35% manual-start band, so the overnight run at 01:20 was correctly triggered. The 20% hard floor was never threatened. Sunset SOC of 43% was low, which explains the two back-to-back evening runs beginning at 18:00.

Three genset runs totalling 4.42 hours consumed approximately 143.7 L of diesel at PHP 86.50/L, a daily fuel cost near PHP 12,430. The two consecutive evening runs - 18:00 to 19:10 (SOC 37 to 58) and 19:15 to 21:00 (SOC 60 to 92) - with only a 5-minute gap look like a premature stop followed by an immediate restart; consolidating these into a single 18:00 to 21:00 run would eliminate unnecessary engine starts and reduce maintenance wear.

2026-05-27.commentary:
May 27 delivered 578.4 kWh of solar, the strongest day in the current dataset and about 58% above the Siargao annual-average baseline. Despite this excellent harvest, the genset contributed 452.5 kWh out of 1,030.9 kWh total - a 44% share - driven by the highest recorded hotel consumption of 856 kWh. That load figure warrants a check: either occupancy spiked or a large piece of equipment ran all day.

The battery reached a dangerous low of 21.7% SOC, just 1.7 percentage points above the 20% hard floor - the closest call in the log. Depth of discharge was 70.5 points. Sunset SOC of only 23.7% confirms the bank was nearly exhausted by late afternoon, which is why the 17:25 genset run was triggered at 22% SOC. This is uncomfortably close to a damaging low-SOC event and should not be repeated.

Six-plus hours of genset across three runs burned approximately 208.7 L of diesel at PHP 86.50/L, a daily fuel cost near PHP 18,050 - the highest in the log. The root cause is the battery being allowed to drain to 22% before the afternoon run started. The concrete fix: treat 28% as the absolute floor for starting the genset, not 20%; if the bank is falling fast in the afternoon, start earlier rather than waiting for the low-SOC alarm.

2026-06-01.commentary:
Solar delivered 485.3 kWh on June 1 -- a reasonable output for early June in Siargao. The diesel genset contributed 360.1 kWh, representing about 42.6% of total generation across 5.33 hours of runtime.

Battery cycled from a low of 28.3% to a high of 97.7%, a depth of discharge of 69.4 percentage points. The minimum SOC touched right at the lower edge of the 28-35% manual-start band.

At 5.33 hours of runtime, diesel consumption was roughly 173 L, costing approximately PHP 14,983. The morning run from 08:35 to 10:25 coincides with rising PV output -- consider delaying that start to save 0.5-1 hour of diesel.

2026-06-02.commentary:
Solar produced 410.5 kWh on June 2 -- slightly below the prior day -- with the genset adding 293.2 kWh for a combined supply of 703.7 kWh. Diesel accounted for about 41.7% of total generation.

Battery SOC ranged from 28.3% to 93.0%, a depth of discharge of 64.7 percentage points. The minimum again landed at 28.3%, at the lower edge of the 28-35% manual-start band.

The 4.42-hour runtime consumed roughly 143.6 L of diesel at an estimated cost of PHP 12,426. The early morning run (05:05-06:00) at 42% SOC with sunrise imminent could be suppressed to save roughly PHP 2,500.

2026-06-03.commentary:
Solar delivered 405.7 kWh, below the 550 kWh typical for clear June days. The genset contributed 425.4 kWh -- just over 51% of total supply -- driven by an unusually heavy three-run schedule totaling 7.17 hours.

Battery cycled from 93.5% down to 31.7%, a depth of discharge of 61.8 points. The low of 31.7% sat just above the 28-35% manual-start band; the three runs, including a brief 05:00 correction, suggest the overnight charge overshoot triggered an unnecessary predawn restart.

At 7.17 genset hours and 32.5 L/hr, diesel burn was roughly 233 L at approximately PHP 20,145 -- more than double a normal day. The concrete fix is to cap the evening charge target at 90% SOC so the predawn 01:00-04:45 run is eliminated and fuel cost drops by half.

2026-06-04.commentary:
Solar produced 541.3 kWh, a strong day reflecting good June irradiance and low cloud cover. The genset ran a single 3-hour evening session supplying 207.3 kWh -- about 28% of combined energy -- which is the normal pattern for this station.

Battery ranged from 32.3% to 91%, a depth of discharge of 58.7 points. The 32.3% minimum cleared the 28-35% manual-start band without reaching the 20% alarm floor, and the 48% sunset SOC provided a healthy overnight buffer.

Three genset hours burned roughly 97.5 L at an estimated PHP 8,434. The 48% sunset SOC suggests the battery could sustain load until 19:30-20:00 before needing the genset; pushing the evening start 60-90 minutes later would trim about 1 hour of diesel per day.

2026-06-05.commentary:
Solar produced 536.1 kWh, consistent with June norms and close to the prior day, indicating steady irradiance. The genset ran 3.08 hours and supplied 215.8 kWh, covering about 29% of total energy in a single evening run.

Battery cycled from 91% down to 30.7%, a depth of discharge of 60.3 points. The 30.7% minimum sat at the lower edge of the 28-35% manual-start band; the 20% alarm floor was not breached, though the margin was only 10.7 points.

Estimated diesel consumption was about 100 L at roughly PHP 8,659. The evening start triggered at 18:55 with SOC near 31%; raising the SOC start threshold to 35% would widen the safety margin and reduce the risk of a low-SOC alarm on heavier-load evenings.

2026-06-06.commentary:
Solar delivered 507.1 kWh, slightly softer than the prior two days but still a solid mid-June result with a 89.88 kW peak. The genset ran a single 3-hour evening session contributing 204.7 kWh -- about 29% of combined supply.

Battery depth of discharge was 58.0 points, cycling from 91.3% down to 33.3%. The 33.3% minimum sat within the 28-35% manual-start band above the 20% floor, and the 44.7% sunset SOC was a reasonable overnight entry point.

Three genset hours consumed roughly 97.5 L of diesel at an estimated PHP 8,434. The consistent 18:30 fixed start time may be triggering earlier than necessary on good solar days; replacing it with a SOC threshold at 35% would let the battery coast longer on clear days and cut runtime.

2026-06-07.commentary:
Solar produced 566.7 kWh, the strongest day of this 5-day window, indicating excellent irradiance for early June. Despite the high solar yield, the genset still ran 3.17 hours supplying 219.8 kWh -- about 28% of combined energy -- to cover the evening demand gap.

Battery hit a minimum of 28.7%, right at the bottom of the 28-35% manual-start band and only 8.7 points above the 20% critical alarm floor. Depth of discharge was 62.8 points, the deepest of the five days captured, with the genset starting at 18:30 when SOC was already at 30%.

Diesel consumption was roughly 103 L at an estimated PHP 8,910. The 28.7% near-miss is a clear warning: raise the evening genset start threshold to 37% SOC to provide a 17-point buffer above the alarm floor and prevent a potential deep-discharge event on high-load nights.

2026-06-08.commentary:
Solar produced 578.7 kWh, the strongest single-day output of the week, with a 77.77 kW peak indicating solid midday irradiance. The genset ran a single 3.58-hour evening session contributing 229.5 kWh -- about 28% of combined supply.

Battery cycled from 93% down to 30.3%, a depth of discharge of 62.7 points. The genset triggered at 17:40 with SOC at only 30% -- the earliest evening start of the week -- and the 31.8% sunset SOC shows the battery was essentially at its low point right at dusk.

At 3.58 genset hours, diesel burn was roughly 116 L at approximately PHP 10,069. The 17:40 start with SOC at 30% confirms evening drain is outpacing the sunset window; raising the start threshold to 37% and reviewing afternoon load would prevent the battery touching 30% before dark.

2026-06-09.commentary:
Solar delivered 575.6 kWh, consistent with recent days and reflecting steady June irradiance. The genset ran two separate sessions totaling 4.83 hours and supplying 315.5 kWh -- about 35% of combined energy -- a higher-than-normal diesel share driven by the predawn run.

Battery ranged from 37.7% to 93%, a depth of discharge of 55.3 points. The predawn run started at 03:10 with SOC at 39%, within the manual-start band but unusual at that hour; the morning boost resulted in a healthy 65.3% sunset SOC. The evening run triggered at 43% SOC -- better than recent days.

Combined diesel consumption was roughly 157 L at an estimated PHP 13,580. The 03:10 predawn start at 39% SOC is worth investigating: if it was automatic, the overnight discharge rate from HVAC or refrigeration may be higher than expected; confirming whether this was manual or auto would clarify whether a schedule or load adjustment is needed.

2026-06-10.commentary:
Solar delivered 556.1 kWh while the diesel genset contributed 344.9 kWh, placing the genset share at roughly 38 percent of the 901 kWh total supply. Peak PV reached 94.45 kW, a strong clear-sky output for mid-June Siargao as the wet season begins. The elevated diesel fraction suggests intermittent cloud cover and two deliberate evening runs pulled the genset in for a combined 5 hours.

The battery cycled from a high of 89.8 percent down to a minimum of 26.2 percent, a depth of discharge of 63.6 percentage points. The 26.2 percent trough sits below the 28 percent lower edge of the recommended manual-start band, meaning the morning genset run at 04:15 fired just after the battery slipped beneath that threshold. The 20 percent safety floor was not breached, so no risk to cell longevity this day.

Five genset hours at roughly 43 L/hr burned an estimated 215 litres of diesel, costing approximately PHP 18,598 at PHP 86.50 per litre. The evening run extended to 20:00 and reached 90 percent SOC, which is higher than needed. Consider setting an automatic stop at 80 percent SOC during evening runs to save roughly 0.5 to 1 hour of diesel per cycle.

2026-06-11.commentary:
Solar delivered 441.5 kWh against a measured load of 758 kWh, with the genset supplying 495.9 kWh -- roughly 53 pct of total supply. Peak PV hit 76.6 kW, a solid mid-June output for the site, though the heavy genset share signals either high occupancy or limited sun hours.

The battery swung from a low of 29.2 pct to a full charge of 99 pct, a depth of discharge of 69.8 percentage points. The 29.2 pct minimum sits just inside the 28-35 pct manual-start band, meaning the control strategy is using the battery close to its target floor without breaching the 20 pct hard limit.

At 43 L/hr over 7.75 hours the genset consumed roughly 333 L of diesel, costing approximately PHP 28,825 at PHP 86.50/L. The 08:30-11:10 run overlaps with peak solar production; shifting that charge session earlier to around 07:30 would let the genset ramp down before midday PV peaks and reduce fuel spend meaningfully.

2026-06-12.commentary:
Solar delivered 528.4 kWh -- the strongest PV day so far in June -- with peak output reaching 91.5 kW, suggesting good sun conditions on the island. Even so, the genset contributed 530.3 kWh, roughly 50 pct of total supply, against a measured load of 923 kWh, pointing to high occupancy or elevated equipment demand.

The battery ranged from 33.5 pct to 93 pct, a depth of discharge of 59.5 percentage points. The 33.5 pct minimum sits right at the top of the 28-35 pct manual-start band, meaning the control strategy triggered the genset at the intended threshold and kept the battery above the 20 pct hard floor all day. Sunset SOC came in at a healthy 56.2 pct.

At 43 L/hr over 7.67 hours the genset consumed roughly 330 L of diesel, costing approximately PHP 28,545. With sunset SOC already at 56 pct there is room to push that higher: scheduling a pre-sunset charge run that ends by 17:30 and targets 70 pct SOC would reduce the need for late-evening genset starts and trim overnight fuel spend.

2026-06-13.commentary:
Solar contributed 464.7 kWh with a peak of 93.2 kW, a strong output for mid-June. The genset added 488 kWh -- roughly 51 pct of total supply -- against a measured load of 822 kWh. Four separate genset runs across the day point to sustained high demand that battery and solar alone could not cover.

The battery ranged from 31 pct to 95.8 pct, a depth of discharge of 64.8 percentage points. The 31 pct minimum sits within the 28-35 pct manual-start band and comfortably above the 20 pct hard floor. Sunset SOC reached 76 pct -- the best of the week -- meaning the battery entered the night well-charged and night-time genset pressure should have been lower.

At 43 L/hr over 7.83 hours the genset burned roughly 337 L of diesel, costing approximately PHP 29,140. The 13:15-16:15 midday run is the most avoidable: solar was near its daily peak during that window, so the genset was competing with PV rather than complementing it. Raising the midday SOC start threshold from 32 pct to 25 pct during solar hours would let PV recover the battery naturally and eliminate roughly 2-3 hours of fuel burn per day.

2026-06-14.commentary:
Solar delivered 467.5 kWh with the genset adding 368.3 kWh -- about 44 pct of total supply -- against a load of 873 kWh. This is a better solar-to-genset ratio than the preceding days, suggesting good sun hours and lower morning demand pressure.

Battery ranged from 33.2 pct to 92.2 pct, a depth of discharge of 59 percentage points. The 33.2 pct minimum sits at the top of the 28-35 pct manual-start band, indicating the system is triggering genset starts close to the intended threshold. Sunset SOC of 70.2 pct provided a solid overnight buffer.

At 43 L/hr over 5.25 hours the genset burned roughly 226 L of diesel, costing approximately PHP 19,540. The 23:30 run was brief and pushed SOC only to 48 pct -- a short top-up that could be deferred to early morning when it can run longer and more efficiently. Setting a minimum run duration of 45 minutes for overnight starts would avoid these short cycles.

2026-06-15.commentary:
Solar contributed 455.6 kWh and the genset 356.4 kWh -- 44 pct of total supply -- against a load of 818 kWh. This is the second consecutive day with genset share below 45 pct, a meaningful improvement over the 50-53 pct range seen earlier in the week.

Battery swung from 36 pct to 92.7 pct, a depth of discharge of 56.7 percentage points. The 36 pct minimum is just above the manual-start band, suggesting genset starts were triggered slightly later than the target threshold. Sunset SOC reached 81.8 pct -- the best of the week -- providing excellent overnight coverage.

At 43 L/hr over 5.25 hours the genset burned roughly 226 L, costing approximately PHP 19,540. With only 2 runs and the strongest sunset SOC of the week, June 15 is the most efficient day recorded so far. The main remaining opportunity is the 15:15 afternoon run -- shifting it 30-45 minutes later to let afternoon PV push SOC higher first could shave 30-60 minutes of fuel burn per day.

2026-06-16.commentary:
Solar delivered 552.2 kWh -- the highest daily PV output recorded this month -- with the genset contributing only 300.2 kWh, or about 35 pct of total supply. Against a load of 778 kWh this is the strongest solar day yet, continuing the downward trend in genset reliance that began on June 14.

Battery ranged from 37.5 pct to 93 pct, a depth of discharge of 55.5 percentage points. The 37.5 pct minimum sits just above the manual-start band and the system managed the full day with only 2 genset runs. Sunset SOC of 53.3 pct is decent, though lower than June 15's 81.8 pct, likely reflecting higher late-afternoon demand.

At 43 L/hr over 4.42 hours the genset burned roughly 190 L of diesel, costing approximately PHP 16,440 -- the lowest fuel day of the week by a wide margin. The evening run (18:30-21:20) is the main remaining cost driver; pushing sunset SOC above 70 pct via a brief pre-sunset charge on good solar days like this one could shorten or eliminate that run and cut nightly fuel spend further.

2026-06-17.notes: Auto-captured from DeyeCloud station 61801841. PARTIAL DAY DATA: 236/289 records returned; totals may be understated.
2026-06-17.commentary:
June 17 returned partial data - 236 of 289 records - so totals are likely undercounts. Captured solar 395 kWh and genset 309 kWh, a 44 percent genset share. Peak PV reached 95.7 kW indicating good sun while monitoring was working.

Battery min was 29.2 percent at the bottom of the 28-35 percent manual-start band, clear of the 20 percent floor; depth of discharge 63 points. Sunset SOC of 85.5 percent was unusually high, explained by the daytime genset run pushing the bank to 92 percent.

The 13:35 to 16:55 daytime run is the avoidable cost - starting at 29 percent during good solar and charging to 92 percent burned roughly 145 L of diesel during a window the array should have handled. Same rule as May: no daytime genset starts while solar is producing well.

2026-06-18.commentary:
June 18 was the strongest solar day of the week at 576 kWh, but genset still added 390 kWh - a 40 percent share - driven by an elevated load of 857 kWh. That load figure is unusually high and worth checking against occupancy or equipment.

Battery cycled 29 to 94 percent, depth of discharge 65 points. The 29 percent minimum sat at the bottom of the manual-start band, well above the 20 percent floor. Sunset SOC 53 percent gave modest overnight buffer.

Two runs totaling 5.67 hours cost an estimated PHP 21,000. The 05:00 morning run was justified by the overnight low; the 18:50 evening run was needed for the night. Both runs charged past 90 percent - stopping at 80-85 percent would save 30-60 minutes per run, per fault F6.

2026-06-19.commentary:
June 19 was the most efficient day of the captured period. Solar 475 kWh and genset only 198 kWh - a 29 percent share - with just one genset run. Load was 720 kWh, the lowest of the week, helping the favorable ratio.

Battery min of 33.8 percent sat above the manual-start band; depth of discharge 59 points. Sunset SOC of 57 percent gave healthy overnight buffer. The bank reached 92.8 percent max - solar carried daytime charging cleanly.

The single 16:30 to 19:25 run lifted SOC from 35 to 92 percent at an estimated PHP 10,800. This is the operating pattern to replicate every day: one evening run triggered at a real low point, solar doing the daytime work. The remaining tweak is stopping at 80-85 percent per fault F6.

2026-06-20.notes: Auto-captured from DeyeCloud station 61801841. CRITICAL: Min SOC 24.8 percent - similar to May 27 fault F4.
2026-06-20.commentary:
June 20 brought the most dangerous battery event since May 27. Solar produced a respectable 510 kWh but battery SOC fell to 24.8 percent - only 4.8 points above the 20 percent hard floor and well below the 28-35 percent manual-start band. Genset contributed 422 kWh, a 45 percent share, across 6.5 hours and roughly PHP 24,200 in diesel.

Depth of discharge was 66 points. The pre-dawn run from 01:35 to 04:15 pushed SOC from 38 to 90 percent - useful but oddly timed, likely contributing to afternoon curtailment. Sunset SOC of 37 percent was already weak going into the evening.

The critical issue: the evening genset starting at 26 percent SOC at 17:55 - below the agreed band. Enforce the May 27 fault F4 rule: start the evening genset no later than 28 percent SOC, and if the bank is falling fast after 15:00, start sooner rather than waiting for an alarm.

2026-06-21.commentary:
June 21 continued the heavy-genset pattern of June 20. Solar 490 kWh, genset 412 kWh - a 46 percent share - across 6.08 hours for an estimated PHP 22,600 in diesel. Load remained elevated at 803 kWh.

Battery min of 29.3 percent sat at the bottom of the manual-start band; depth of discharge 62 points. The bank reached 91.5 percent max and sunset SOC was 45.8 percent, both reasonable.

Two runs: 05:10 to 08:00 lifting SOC from 31 to 91 percent (long morning), and 18:30 to 21:45 from 31 to 92 percent (evening). Both ran past 90 percent, continuing the fault F6 pattern. With load near 800 kWh several days running, the spec sheet load assumptions need revisiting - this is structural, not operational.

2026-06-22.commentary:
Solar delivered 486.9 kWh while the diesel genset contributed 241.8 kWh, together supplying 728.7 kWh total. The genset covered roughly 33% of all energy consumed, a high share for mid-June in Siargao - likely driven by afternoon cloud cover and sustained evening demand stretching into midnight.

The battery cycled from a high of 93% down to a minimum of 30.5%, a depth of discharge of 62.5 percentage points. The low of 30.5% sits at the very top of the 28-35% manual-start band, meaning the afternoon genset run was triggered at the right threshold. The second run beginning at 22:55 kept the bank from falling further overnight.

At 3.58 genset hours, diesel consumption was approximately 154 L costing roughly PHP 13,321 at PHP 86.50/L. The late-night run (22:55 to midnight) is the key cost driver. Moving interruptible loads such as laundry or water heating into midday solar hours could trim one to two hours of after-dark runtime and reduce diesel spend by PHP 3,700 to PHP 7,400 on similar days.

2026-06-23.commentary:
Solar yielded 418.4 kWh and the genset contributed 282.7 kWh for a combined 701.1 kWh total. The diesel share was 40%, the highest in recent days, pointing to reduced irradiance - possibly heavy cloud cover or intermittent rain typical of mid-June in Siargao. Peak solar hit only 81.5 kW, well below the array capacity.

The battery ranged from 91.3% down to 29.7%, a depth of discharge of 61.6 percentage points. The minimum of 29.7% sits just inside the 28-35% manual-start band, so the afternoon genset trigger at 16:35 was timely. The midnight carryover run (00:00-01:00) from the previous evening left the bank at 84% heading into the morning, which helped delay the afternoon start.

The genset logged 4.17 hours across two runs, burning approximately 179 L of diesel at a cost of roughly PHP 15,494. The afternoon run (16:35-19:45) accounts for most of the runtime. Charging the battery closer to 95-100% before 15:00 on overcast days - by briefly running the genset at noon when load is low - could push the evening start time later and shorten total runtime by an estimated hour.

2026-06-24.commentary:
Solar generation reached 456 kWh on June 24, a solid output for mid-rainy season with peak array output hitting 84.61 kW. The diesel genset contributed 349.2 kWh, representing roughly 43% of total energy supply for the day. Load consumption logged 701 kWh, well within combined generation capacity.

The battery cycled from a high of 92.5% down to a low of 33.2%, a depth of discharge of 59.3 percentage points. The minimum SOC of 33.2% sits just inside the 28-35% manual-start band, meaning the genset triggered appropriately before reaching the 20% protection floor. Two separate genset runs - overnight and in the evening - prevented further depletion.

The genset ran 5.17 hours, burning roughly 222 liters of diesel at approximately PHP 86.50 per liter for a fuel cost near PHP 19,200. This is a high fuel spend driven by two full charge cycles from mid-SOC. To cut costs, consider raising the auto-start threshold to 38% and the stop threshold to 95% so each run extracts maximum charge per start.

2026-06-25.commentary:
Solar generation on June 25 reached 416.2 kWh, a reasonable output for mid-year in Siargao where cloud cover increases during the wet season. The genset contributed 402.7 kWh, representing 49% of total supply (819 kWh combined) -- a high diesel share indicating backup power was needed for much of the day. Peak PV hit 89 kW, suggesting some strong sun windows, but overcast periods reduced solar yield significantly.

The battery swung from 24.3% up to a full 99%, a depth of discharge of 74.7 percentage points. The minimum SOC of 24.3% dipped below the 28-35% manual-start band and approached the 20% hard floor, meaning the battery was nearly depleted before the first genset run at 06:25. Three separate genset events across the day indicate the battery kept falling back toward the lower threshold after each recharge cycle.

Six hours and 45 minutes of genset runtime consumed approximately 290 liters of diesel at an estimated cost of PHP 25,100 for the day. The early-morning run (06:25-09:45) is the most expensive pattern -- overnight discharge draining the battery before solar can recover it. Raising the overnight minimum SOC setpoint to 40% would likely eliminate at least one of the three starts and reduce daily diesel spend by an estimated 15-20%.

Genset ran 8.58 hours across 3 windows for an estimated PHP 31,926 in diesel (369 L). Genset share above 45 percent indicates weak solar or elevated load - worth investigating which.

2026-06-28.commentary:
On June 28, solar generated 540.4 kWh against a total load of 899 kWh, a solid performance for the wet season. The diesel genset contributed 426.4 kWh, or roughly 44% of total supply, across two separate run cycles totaling 6.17 hours -- one overnight (01:00-03:55) and one in the evening (17:30-20:45).

The battery cycled between 27.5% and 91.8%, a depth of discharge of 64.3 percentage points. The 27.5% minimum dipped just below the 28% manual-start lower bound, meaning the evening genset triggered right at the floor of the target band. Sunset SOC was only 28%, leaving minimal buffer heading into the night demand period.

At 43 L per hour and PHP 86.50 per liter, the 6.17-hour runtime cost approximately PHP 22,950 in diesel. To reduce this, shift high-draw loads (laundry, water heating) into the solar peak window of 10:00-14:00 when PV approached its 92.92 kW peak, which would preserve battery SOC longer into the evening and push back genset start time.

2026-06-29.commentary:
June 29 was a very poor solar day with PV generation of only 65.6 kWh against a total consumption of roughly 655 kWh - the diesel genset supplied about 90% of all energy. Peak PV reached only 20.19 kW, pointing to heavy cloud cover consistent with Siargao wet-season conditions. Solar is contributing minimally at this point in the rainy season.

The battery cycled through a depth of 59.6 percentage points, from a low of 32.7% up to a high of 92.3%. All three genset starts triggered in the 34-35% SOC range, sitting at the top of the 28-35% manual-start band and well above the 20% floor - appropriate triggering behaviour. The battery was recharged to around 91-92% on each cycle, which is efficient per run.

Three genset runs totaling 8.58 hours burned roughly 369 liters of diesel at approximately PHP 31,918 for the day - a very high operating cost driven almost entirely by cloud cover. The most impactful single change would be to consolidate the three runs into two by extending the midday window until the afternoon dip is absorbed, reducing daily start cycles and engine wear without increasing fuel burn.

2026-06-30.commentary:
Solar output on June 30 was moderate at 517 kWh with a peak PV draw of 80.7 kW, but the diesel genset carried a heavy 48.2 percent share of total generation (481.4 kWh of the 998.4 kWh combined). That is a high genset contribution for a day with a decent peak PV reading, pointing to either a run of cloud cover through the day or load demand outpacing what the array could deliver, especially given genset runs both overnight and in the evening.

Battery cycling was deep: SOC swung from a minimum of 30.5 percent up to 93 percent, a depth of discharge of 62.5 points. The 30.5 percent floor sits inside the 28 to 35 percent manual-start band, about 10.5 points above the 20 percent hard floor, so the system is running close to where an operator would be expected to kick the genset on manually rather than relying on automatic cutoffs.

The genset ran about 5.0 hours across four separate starts, burning roughly 215 liters of diesel (5.0 hrs x 43 L per hr) at an estimated cost of PHP 18,598 (215 L x PHP 86.50 per L) for the day. Four separate start-stop cycles, especially the three short overnight and evening runs, suggest the genset is being toggled on and off rather than run in one longer, more efficient block - consolidating those starts into a single scheduled run would cut fuel waste from repeated startup ramps.

2026-07-01.commentary:
Solar production on July 1 came in at 388.7 kWh, a moderate day for the season with peak PV output reaching 81.65 kW. The diesel genset contributed 402.5 kWh against total generation of 791.2 kWh, putting the diesel share at roughly 51 percent of the day energy mix, a heavier reliance on genset than solar alone would suggest was necessary.

Battery cycling showed a depth of discharge of 60.0 percentage points, swinging between a low of 38.2 percent and a high of 98.2 percent state of charge. The minimum SOC of 38.2 percent stayed comfortably above both the 20 percent hard floor and the 28-35 percent manual-start band, meaning the battery never approached a level that would have required genset intervention on its own.

The genset ran three separate times for a combined 3.08 hours, burning an estimated 132.4 liters of diesel at roughly PHP 11,456 for the day. Since SOC never dropped into the manual-start band during any of the three runs, these look like scheduled or load-driven starts rather than emergency top-ups; reviewing the trigger logic for the mid-morning and evening runs could likely shave one cycle off the day and cut diesel spend without touching guest comfort.

2026-07-02.commentary:
Solar production on July 2 was strong for the season, with the array generating 531.6 kWh against a load of about 723 kWh. The diesel genset contributed 237.6 kWh, or roughly 31 percent of total generation (pv plus genset), meaning the sun still carried close to 70 percent of the day's energy needs.

Battery cycling was deep this day, with a depth of discharge of 70.0 percentage points (max SoC 90.5%, min SoC 20.5%). The pack dipped to just 20.5%, essentially touching the 20% floor and well below the 28-35% manual-start band, indicating the genset was called in later than ideal and the battery was allowed to run closer to its protective limit than ops would like.

The genset ran 3.17 hours starting at 18:55 with SoC already at 21%, burning about 136.3 liters of diesel at an estimated cost of roughly PHP 11,790 for the day. Starting the genset earlier, as soon as SoC crosses into the 28-35% band rather than waiting until it nears 20%, would shorten genset runtime, ease strain on the battery, and likely trim fuel spend over the week.

2026-07-03.commentary:
Solar production on July 3 came in at 515 kWh, with the genset contributing 249 kWh -- about 32.6 percent of total generation. Peak PV output reached 93.53 kW around midday, showing the array can perform well when conditions cooperate, but the genset still had to cover both a morning window (08:35-11:20) and an evening window (19:05-21:40), pointing to softer solar coverage on the shoulders of the day rather than a strong, uniformly sunny day for the season.

Battery cycling was deep, with a depth of discharge of 68.7 percentage points (SOC ranged from 30.3 percent up to 99 percent). The minimum SOC of 30.3 percent stayed above the 20 percent hard floor, giving about 10 points of headroom, but it landed right inside the 28-35 percent manual-start band, meaning the genset dispatch that day was consistent with the planned manual-start trigger rather than an emergency low-battery event.

The two genset runs totaled 5.33 hours, burning roughly 229 liters of diesel (5.33 hrs x 43 L/hr) at an estimated cost of about PHP 19,825 (229 L x PHP 86.50/L). The morning run is the one worth examining: it started at 08:35 while SOC was already at 33 percent, ahead of the sun reaching its later 93.53 kW peak. Delaying the manual genset start slightly later in the morning, or confirming the load profile does not need full support that early, could trim one of the two daily runs and meaningfully cut diesel spend.

Solar produced 536 kWh and the diesel genset added 397 kWh, putting genset at 43 percent of system energy against a load of 853 kWh.

Battery min of 31.0 percent sat within the 28-35 percent manual-start band - timing was on target. Depth of discharge was 62 points; max SOC reached 93 percent.

Genset ran 5.58 hours across 3 windows for an estimated PHP 20,767 in diesel (240 L).

2026-07-05.commentary:
Solar production on July 5 came in solid at 580.4 kWh, with a strong midday peak of 87.7 kW showing the array was performing well. Even so, the diesel genset had to carry 296.2 kWh of the days energy, which works out to about 33.8 percent of total generation - a meaningful chunk given how good the solar peak looked, pointing to either high overall load (peak of 92.0 kW) or gaps in coverage outside peak sun hours.

Battery cycling was deep, swinging from a low of 32.2 percent SOC up to 92 percent, a depth of discharge near 59.8 points. The 32.2 percent minimum stayed clear of the 20 percent hard floor by about 12 points, but it sat right at the edge of the 28-35 percent manual-start band, meaning the genset was likely brought on manually as SOC drifted down rather than being forced by an automatic low-SOC trip.

The genset ran three separate times for 4.17 hours total, burning roughly 179 liters of diesel at PHP 86.50 per liter for a fuel cost near PHP 15,500 for the day. The longest run, 22:05 to 24:05, covered overnight load with zero solar available - shifting some of that overnight draw to the battery, or trimming overnight loads, would let that stretch run shorter or be skipped, cutting into the single biggest cost driver of the day.

2026-07-06.commentary:
Solar production on July 6 was solid for the season at 536.6 kWh, a healthy day of PV output. The diesel genset added 285.0 kWh on top of that, meaning generator power covered about 34.7 percent of total site generation (285 divided by 536.6 plus 285). That is a moderate reliance on diesel for a day with strong sun, pointing to evening load or a schedule that brought the genset on before solar was fully depleted.

Battery cycling was moderate, with SOC swinging from a low of 35.7 percent up to a high of 93 percent, a depth of discharge of 57.3 percentage points. The 35.7 percent floor stayed comfortably above the 20 percent hard limit and sat just above the 28-35 percent manual-start band, so the battery was never pushed into the zone where an operator would need to step in manually. This is a healthy cycling range that avoids deep-discharge stress on the bank.

The genset ran three separate times for a combined 4.17 hours, burning roughly 179.3 liters of diesel (4.17 hr times 43 L per hr) at an estimated cost of about PHP 15,510 (179.3 L times PHP 86.50 per L). The short 00:00 to 01:00 run stands out: SOC was already at 79 percent when it started, so that run added little value for the diesel spent. Reviewing or removing that early-morning start condition would be the single easiest way to cut fuel cost without affecting battery health.

2026-07-08.commentary:
Solar output on July 8 was moderate at 494.6 kWh, with a strong peak of 88.53 kW reflecting good midday sun. Even so, the genset supplied 251.3 kWh, about 33.7 percent of the days total generation - a similar diesel share to recent days, driven by a high peak load of 99.8 kW that likely outran what the array and battery alone could cover at times.

Battery cycling was very deep, running from a minimum of 32.3 percent SOC up to 99 percent, a depth of discharge of about 66.7 points. The 32.3 percent floor cleared the 20 percent hard limit by roughly 12 points but sat right at the top edge of the 28-35 percent manual-start band, so the low point of the day is consistent with a manual genset start rather than an automatic low-SOC trip.

The genset logged nine separate runs totaling 5.33 hours, or about 229 liters of diesel at PHP 86.50 per liter - close to PHP 19,800 for the day. Several of those runs were very short bursts around midday (11:10-11:45 and 15:25-15:30) while SOC was already 80-98 percent, which looks like short-cycling rather than genuine need; tightening the genset start and stop thresholds so it does not fire during periods of high SOC and strong solar would cut wear and shave meaningful fuel cost.

2026-07-09.notes: Auto-captured from DeyeCloud station 61801841. Data feed covered 249 of 289 expected intervals (cuts off around 20:45).
2026-07-09.commentary:
July 9 was a weak solar day: only 180.4 kWh of pv production with a peak of just 65.28 kW, well below the mid-80s kW peaks seen on better days. With that little sun to work with, the genset had to cover 414.2 kWh, about 69.7 percent of total generation - by far the heaviest diesel dependence of the days reviewed here, consistent with a cloudy or rainy day.

Battery cycling was shallower than recent days, moving from 37.2 percent SOC up to 83.3 percent, a depth of discharge of about 46.1 points. The 37.2 percent minimum stayed above both the 20 percent hard floor and the 28-35 percent manual-start band, meaning the battery never actually dropped low enough to trigger a start on SOC alone - the genset ran mainly to make up for the shortfall in solar, not because the battery was running out.

The genset ran six times for 6.33 hours total, burning about 272 liters of diesel at PHP 86.50 per liter, close to PHP 23,550 for the day - the most expensive of the days captured. Note also that the data feed for this day only covered 249 of the expected 289 five-minute intervals, cutting off around 20:45; that gap is worth checking on DeyeCloud, and going forward, consolidating the six separate genset starts into fewer, longer runs would reduce start-cycle wear on an already heavy-use day.

2026-07-12.commentary:
Solar produced 475 kWh and the diesel genset added 310 kWh, putting genset at 40 percent of system energy against a load of 760 kWh.

Battery min of 29.3 percent sat within the 28-35 percent manual-start band - timing was on target. Depth of discharge was 70 points; max SOC reached 99 percent.

Genset ran 4.00 hours across 4 windows for an estimated PHP 14,878 in diesel (172 L).

2026-07-13.commentary:
Solar output was moderate but not exceptional for July in Siargao: 531 kWh of PV production against 400.1 kWh from the diesel genset, meaning the genset carried about 43 percent of total energy delivered that day. Peak PV briefly touched 90.5 kW near midday, so the array clearly had capacity, but average output stayed well below that peak, pointing to intermittent cloud cover cutting into potential yield.

Battery cycling was fairly deep, running from 97.7 percent down to 32.5 percent for a depth of discharge around 65 points. The floor sits at 20 percent, so the bank never got close to a hard cutoff, but the 32.5 percent minimum landed squarely inside the 28 to 35 percent manual-start band, which is exactly when the operator would be expected to bring the genset online.

The genset logged 4.25 hours of runtime, which at roughly 43 L per hr works out to about 183 liters of diesel, or close to PHP 15,800 at PHP 86.50 per liter. The log shows two very short 5-minute blips around 19:50 and 20:00 before the real evening run started at 20:10 - those short cycles add wear and startup fuel for no real output, so it is worth checking whether those were false starts or load-test blips, and consolidating into a single clean start would save some diesel and engine hours.

2026-07-14.commentary:
Solar production on July 14 came in at 341.6 kWh with a peak output of 71.17 kW, well short of covering the day's total load of 801 kWh. The diesel genset had to fill the gap with 533.6 kWh, meaning diesel supplied roughly 61 percent of total generation for the day. That is a heavy reliance on backup power and points to a weak solar day relative to demand.

Battery cycling was deep, swinging from a peak state of charge near 99 percent down to a low of 27 percent, a depth of discharge of about 72 percentage points. The 27 percent low sits below the 28 to 35 percent band where operators would normally step in to manually start the genset, though it stayed above the 20 percent hard floor. The battery was allowed to run down past the usual manual-start trigger point before backup power engaged.

The genset ran four separate times for a combined 7 hours, including one very short 5 minute cycle around 14:50 that likely added little more than wear and fuel cost. At roughly 43 liters per hour and PHP 86.50 per liter, 7 hours works out to about 301 liters and PHP 26,037 in diesel spend for the day. One concrete fix: tighten the genset start logic so brief spikes like the 14:50 blip do not trigger a fresh start, folding that demand into the adjacent 13:15 to 14:45 run instead.

2026-07-15.commentary:
Solar produced 569.7 kWh, a decent mid-July result for the wet season though short of what the array delivers in full sun. The diesel genset added 389.5 kWh, so diesel covered about 41 percent of total generation - a heavy diesel day driven by a high site load of roughly 950 kWh.

The battery cycled hard, swinging 65.5 points from a high of 95.3 percent down to 29.8 percent. The minimum stayed above the 20 percent floor but sat right inside the 28-35 percent manual-start band, and a sunset SOC of only 31.5 percent left almost no buffer for the evening, forcing the 17:30 genset start.

The genset ran 5.67 hours, roughly 244 liters of diesel or about PHP 21,100, which works out near PHP 54 per genset kWh. The three short midday runs between 12:50 and 14:00 while solar was still producing were the least efficient fuel use; cutting those and letting PV carry the afternoon, then running one solid evening charge block, would trim cost without risking the floor.

2026-07-16.commentary:
Solar came in at 440.4 kWh, a below-average day for mid-July and clearly cloud-affected despite a strong 88.9 kW peak. The diesel genset supplied 460.5 kWh, which means diesel covered about 51 percent of total generation - the genset out-produced the array for the day.

Battery cycling was deep, swinging 70.5 points from 98.5 percent down to 28 percent. The minimum sat at the very bottom of the 28-35 percent manual-start band and stayed above the 20 percent floor, but both the 01:05 and 18:00 starts were triggered right at the band edge, showing the bank is being leaned on hard between charges.

The genset ran 7.92 hours, roughly 341 liters of diesel or about PHP 29,500, near PHP 64 per genset kWh. Almost three of those hours ran between 11:10 and 13:45 when the array should carry the load; on cloudy days it is still cheaper to let SOC ride down toward the band and do one long evening run, so tightening the midday start rule is the single change most likely to cut fuel.

faults.title: Genset running during active solar production
faults.description: Genset started while SOC was already above the 28-35% manual-start band and solar was actively charging. Burns diesel for energy the sun delivers for free and causes afternoon solar curtailment by pushing battery to 100% before peak sun.
faults.recommendation: Do not start the genset when SOC is above 35% during daylight hours (06:00-17:00). Stop morning runs at 75-80% SOC and let solar complete the charge. Enforce this as a written operating rule with the on-site team.

faults.title: Micro-run and rapid restart events
faults.description: Genset running for 5-10 minutes at near-full SOC (97-99%). These short runs produce almost no useful energy but each start-stop cycle adds wear equivalent to roughly 30 minutes of running. Likely caused by inverter auto-restart on transient load spikes or a misconfigured charge-complete threshold in the inverter/BMS.
faults.recommendation: Check inverter generator control settings and set a minimum genset run time of 30 minutes. Log exact timestamps of these events in the site maintenance book and investigate which loads are causing transient spikes at that hour. Discuss with supplier whether the inverter float/restart threshold needs adjusting.

faults.title: Consecutive evening runs with 5-minute gap (possible auto-shutoff)
faults.description: On May 26, genset ran 18:00-19:10 (SOC 37 to 58%), stopped for 5 minutes, then restarted at 19:15 and ran until 21:00. A 5-minute gap is abnormal. Possible causes: operator stopped prematurely, automatic overtemperature shutdown, or a cooldown timer triggered. Multiple unnecessary engine starts accelerate maintenance intervals.
faults.recommendation: Pull the genset fault log for May 26 evening and check for overtemperature or cooldown alarm at approximately 19:10. If operator-caused, brief team: once a charge window is started, commit to the full stop target (85% SOC). If auto-shutoff, check cooldown timer and thermal protection settings with supplier.

faults.title: Battery depleted to near-critical SOC (21.7% on May 27)
faults.description: Battery SOC reached 21.7% on May 27 - only 1.7 percentage points above the 20% hard-cutoff floor. At the observed afternoon depletion rate, a 15-minute delay would likely have triggered an automatic low-SOC shutdown, cutting power to the hotel. The afternoon genset start at 17:25 was too late.
faults.recommendation: Treat 28% as the absolute minimum SOC for starting the afternoon genset - not 20%. Brief the on-site operator: when SOC is falling rapidly after 15:00 and heading toward 30%, start immediately without waiting for an alarm. This is the highest-priority operational change in this fault log.

faults.title: Abnormally high hotel consumption (856 kWh on May 27)
faults.description: May 27 recorded 856 kWh - 10% above May 26 and approximately 38% above the 7-day average of 622 kWh. This is the highest consumption day in the log. The spike directly contributed to the near-critical battery event (F4). Cause is unknown: could be elevated occupancy, an HVAC unit stuck on, a water heater fault, or kitchen equipment left running continuously.
faults.recommendation: Cross-check hotel occupancy records for May 27. Review BMS and equipment logs for that date. Inspect HVAC units, water heaters, and kitchen equipment for any that may have run uninterrupted. Once the cause is identified, document it in the site log and add a check procedure.

faults.title: Genset overcharging battery beyond optimal stop point
faults.description: Across 6 of 8 days, the genset ran until battery SOC reached 90-100% instead of the optimal 80-85% stop point. The final 15-20% of charge (absorption phase) accepts current very slowly - the genset spends 30-60 extra minutes delivering energy that morning solar would produce for free within 1-2 hours.
faults.recommendation: Program or instruct operators to stop the genset at 80-85% SOC during daylight hours (06:00-17:00). Night runs may target 90%. This change alone could reduce daily genset hours by 0.5-1.0 hr on most days, saving roughly PHP 1,400-2,800 per day in diesel.
